criterion_breakdown.label: Authority-of-record truth preservation
criterion_breakdown.note: Only option that cleanly preserves checkpoint, runtime ledger, settlement ledger, and customer-safe projection as separate machine-readable layers.

criterion_breakdown.label: Raw credential and token isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Concentrates raw credential use inside the vault-plus-gateway boundary and keeps other components on opaque refs.

criterion_breakdown.label: Send-time revalidation and client-binding fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Matches the frozen preflight, binding-lineage, and send-time revalidation rules directly.

criterion_breakdown.label: Callback and inbound ingress safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Supports one ingress checkpoint that authenticates, dedupes, quarantines, and persists AuthorityIngressReceipt before any mutation.

criterion_breakdown.label: Idempotency and duplicate suppression integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Keeps request hashes, duplicate suppression, and canonical receipt refs on the durable command side rather than transport-local memory.

criterion_breakdown.label: Reconciliation and out-of-band correction support
criterion_breakdown.note: Lets reconciliation remain an explicit control-plane path that consumes gateway evidence without collapsing it into transport behavior.

criterion_breakdown.label: No-blind-resend recovery posture
criterion_breakdown.note: Best fit for restore and queue-loss rules because resend legality and ingress proof remain durable and boundary-scoped.

criterion_breakdown.label: Multi-provider evolvability and sandbox or production separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Allows provider-specific headers, callback rules, and sandbox or production partitioning without infecting the whole northbound surface.

criterion_breakdown.label: Observability and audit quality
criterion_breakdown.note: Produces crisp audit lineage across send, ingress, normalization, and reconciliation.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operability, testability, and failure isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Provider degradation, token rotation, and ingress quarantine can fail in the gateway without collapsing the whole command edge.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser, native, and machine-actor trust-boundary clarity
criterion_breakdown.note: Makes it explicit that all callers use the same northbound command contract while only the gateway speaks provider transport.

criterion_breakdown.label: Implementation complexity versus safety payoff
criterion_breakdown.note: Adds complexity, but almost all of it buys legally significant isolation rather than ornamental abstraction.

alternatives.label: Dedicated controlled authority gateway with isolated credential handling, checkpointed ingress, and explicit reconciliation control
risks: Adds an explicit runtime boundary and therefore more inter-service plumbing than an inline transport implementation.
Requires disciplined contract ownership between the gateway, vault, and control plane so the boundary does not degrade into a leaky proxy.
strengths: Best fit for the corpus rule that browser, native, and machine callers must not talk to providers directly.
Preserves the checkpoint-versus-settlement split by letting ingress stop at AuthorityIngressReceipt while the control plane remains the only writer of SubmissionRecord truth.
Keeps raw tokens, client secrets, and sandbox or production profile differences behind a narrow gateway-plus-vault boundary.
alternatives.summary: Keep provider transport, callback ingress, fraud-header composition, and vault-mediated credential usage inside a dedicated controlled gateway, while the manifest control plane owns request identity, submission truth, reconciliation control, and downstream projections.

criterion_breakdown.label: Authority-of-record truth preservation
criterion_breakdown.note: Truth separation can still be implemented, but checkpoint, transport, and settlement logic sit close enough together that drift risk stays materially higher.

criterion_breakdown.label: Raw credential and token isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Vault use remains possible, but raw credential access spreads closer to the main northbound edge.

criterion_breakdown.label: Send-time revalidation and client-binding fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Can support frozen binding checks, though transport convenience pressures the orchestrator to take on too much provider-specific state.

criterion_breakdown.label: Callback and inbound ingress safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Checkpointing is possible, but callback, normalization, and settlement code are easier to collapse together in one process boundary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Idempotency and duplicate suppression integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Durable request identity can exist, but it competes with inline transport shortcuts and service-local retry behavior.

criterion_breakdown.label: Reconciliation and out-of-band correction support
criterion_breakdown.note: Reconciliation still works, but it is more likely to be treated as a branch of inline transport code rather than a durable control path.

criterion_breakdown.label: No-blind-resend recovery posture
criterion_breakdown.note: Recovery can honor resend legality, but coupling transport and orchestration increases accidental resend risk during worker reclaim or rollback.

criterion_breakdown.label: Multi-provider evolvability and sandbox or production separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Provider profile variation is feasible, but adapter concerns leak into the core northbound and orchestration layers.

criterion_breakdown.label: Observability and audit quality
criterion_breakdown.note: Fewer boundaries can simplify local tracing, though audit semantics become less structurally obvious.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operability, testability, and failure isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: A large integrated service is still testable, but provider failure and callback quarantine are less isolated.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser, native, and machine-actor trust-boundary clarity
criterion_breakdown.note: The no-direct-provider rule becomes architectural convention rather than a dedicated enforced boundary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Implementation complexity versus safety payoff
criterion_breakdown.note: Wins on short-term simplicity, though much of that simplicity comes from collapsing boundaries the corpus wants separated.

alternatives.label: Inline authority integration inside the main northbound API and orchestrator boundary
risks: Blurs browser or native session boundaries, request identity logic, provider transport, and callback quarantine inside one large service boundary.
Increases the chance that token use, callback handling, and settlement mutation drift together instead of staying explicitly separated.
strengths: Reduces hop count and can be simpler to deploy initially.
Keeps transport logic close to command authorization and may feel easier for small teams to trace at first.
alternatives.summary: Let the northbound or orchestrator services perform provider transport directly, with request identity, session checks, ingress handling, and reconciliation all co-located in the main application boundary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Authority-of-record truth preservation
criterion_breakdown.note: Checkpoint, settlement, and reconciliation semantics become harder to keep first-class when ingress is mediated by a third-party edge.

criterion_breakdown.label: Raw credential and token isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Hosted integration platforms tend to widen the blast radius of raw credentials and callback secrets compared with a narrow vault-plus-gateway boundary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Send-time revalidation and client-binding fidelity
criterion_breakdown.note: Exact access_binding_hash and policy_snapshot_hash revalidation become less trustworthy once transport is abstracted outside the core runtime boundary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Callback and inbound ingress safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Ingress checkpointing can exist, but legal mutation risks being based on externally normalized events rather than first-party proof packets.

criterion_breakdown.label: Idempotency and duplicate suppression integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Exact request hash and canonical receipt control become harder when delivery identity is shaped by an external platform.

criterion_breakdown.label: Reconciliation and out-of-band correction support
criterion_breakdown.note: External tooling can help fetch provider state, but the bounded reconciliation semantics still need to be rebuilt inside Taxat.

criterion_breakdown.label: No-blind-resend recovery posture
criterion_breakdown.note: Recovery correctness is weakest here because hosted retries and replay semantics are harder to bind to Taxat's resend legality model.

criterion_breakdown.label: Multi-provider evolvability and sandbox or production separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Managed adapters can help with provider heterogeneity, which is the main reason this option remains viable at all.

criterion_breakdown.label: Observability and audit quality
criterion_breakdown.note: Forensic lineage becomes split across first-party and third-party logs.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operability, testability, and failure isolation
criterion_breakdown.note: Adds another operator boundary and makes incident drills depend on external platform behavior the corpus does not control.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser, native, and machine-actor trust-boundary clarity
criterion_breakdown.note: Callers still avoid direct provider traffic, but the decisive boundary shifts away from Taxat's own controlled gateway and vault semantics.

criterion_breakdown.label: Implementation complexity versus safety payoff
criterion_breakdown.note: Can reduce adapter coding, but the safety tradeoff is poor because core truth and recovery invariants remain first-party obligations anyway.

alternatives.label: External managed integration or iPaaS boundary as the primary transport edge
risks: Moves too much legal-state-critical logic outside the control boundary that the corpus describes.
Makes checkpoint proof, exact request lineage, secret isolation, and no-blind-resend recovery materially harder to prove and test end to end.
strengths: Can reduce some custom transport implementation work and offers adapter convenience across providers.
Looks attractive when provider-specific APIs change often or when a platform team wants hosted connector tooling.
alternatives.summary: Push provider transport, callback ingress, and some normalization into an external managed integration platform, with Taxat consuming already-processed events and adapter outcomes from that external edge.

criteria.label: Authority-of-record truth preservation
criteria.rationale: The boundary must preserve the distinct roles of checkpoint, runtime ledger, settlement ledger, workflow coordination, and customer-safe projection so no internal optimism or transport artifact can masquerade as confirmed authority truth.

criteria.label: Raw credential and token isolation
criteria.rationale: Raw authority credentials, client secrets, and signing material must remain behind a governed vault boundary rather than leaking into browser, native, queue, cache, or read-model paths.

criteria.label: Send-time revalidation and client-binding fidelity
criteria.rationale: Every live send must stay bound to the frozen authority lineage, client, subject, environment, access binding, and step-up or approval evidence that originally authorized it, with fail-closed behavior on drift.

criteria.label: Callback and inbound ingress safety
criteria.rationale: Every callback, poll payload, or recovered provider response must be authenticated, deduped, checkpointed, and strongly correlated before mutation, with weak or ambiguous evidence quarantined instead of promoted.

criteria.label: Idempotency and duplicate suppression integrity
criteria.rationale: The architecture must keep request-hash identity, duplicate meaning, idempotency keys, delivery dedupe, and canonical receipt refs as durable control data rather than transient worker memory.

criteria.label: Reconciliation and out-of-band correction support
criteria.rationale: The boundary must keep reconciliation as a first-class control path with persisted budget, ambiguity posture, and reopening semantics so late authority truth or contradictory evidence can safely supersede internal projections.

criteria.label: No-blind-resend recovery posture
criteria.rationale: Queue loss, worker restart, replay, restore, and rollback must recover from persisted ingress, request lineage, and reconciliation control instead of blindly resending live authority mutations.

criteria.label: Multi-provider evolvability and sandbox or production separation
criteria.rationale: Provider transports, profile bindings, callback hosts, and sandbox versus production credentials need one stable boundary that can evolve per provider without collapsing the core control plane or mixing environments.

criteria.label: Observability and audit quality
criteria.rationale: The chosen boundary should produce one explainable audit lineage from initiating actor through request hashing, gateway send, ingress checkpoint, normalization, reconciliation, and downstream projection.

criteria.label: Operability, testability, and failure isolation
criteria.rationale: The design should make provider failure, ingress quarantine, token rotation, and release gating isolatable and testable without turning every northbound request path into provider-coupled runtime logic.

criteria.label: Browser, native, and machine-actor trust-boundary clarity
criteria.rationale: The architecture must keep browser, native, and machine callers away from direct provider traffic so human sessions, service principals, and authority tokens do not collapse into one ambiguous transport edge.

criteria.label: Implementation complexity versus safety payoff
criteria.rationale: The chosen option should add only the complexity needed to protect legal truth, token isolation, and recovery correctness; convenience alone does not justify boundary collapse.

recommended_alternative_label: Dedicated controlled authority gateway with isolated credential handling, checkpointed ingress, and explicit reconciliation control
